Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered most of the time. I'd try to set aside chunks of time during the week, maybe aiming for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that neatly. I found myself doing a lot of reading and writing notes in bursts, especially late at night when my anxiety was keeping me up anyway. I'd download materials and work through them on my own schedule rather than following any strict plan.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and how I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was looming. When my anxiety spiked, I'd either hyperfocus on studying or completely avoid it—there wasn't much middle ground. Deadlines were really the main thing that dictated when I actually sat down to work.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I needed to check if something was due or to download materials I hadn't grabbed yet. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a deadline and that would push me to log in. It wasn't really a habit for me—more like a reactive thing when I remembered I needed something specific from the course.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I checked automatically like social media or email. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it routine, but that fell apart pretty quickly. I think the interface just felt kind of boring to me, and I found it easier to work from downloaded materials instead.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely the most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I liked being able to download PDFs and work through them at my own pace with a highlighter and notes. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the lectures did. Videos were okay but I often found myself zoning out during them.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos after a while—I'd start them but rarely watch the whole thing because my mind would wander. Optional quizzes and extra resources usually got skipped unless I was really struggling with a concept. It wasn't that I thought they were useless, I just felt bored and couldn't motivate myself to engage with stuff that wasn't directly tied to my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it helped clarify something I was genuinely confused about. I needed to see the immediate payoff, otherwise my brain would just check out. If something felt repetitive or like busywork, I couldn't force myself to care about it even when I knew I probably should.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me, even when part of me knew they might help. Required stuff had this weight to it that forced me to at least attempt it, but optional things just got pushed to the bottom of my mental list and stayed there. There's something about knowing it won't affect my grade that makes my anxious brain decide it's not worth the stress.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed notes, downloaded PDFs, and my own handwritten summaries. I'd also use external websites to look up concepts when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notebook is full of pages of rewritten notes that the LMS would have no idea about.
Q10: There were tons of times like this. I'd download a reading once, then spend hours over the next few days working through it and making notes, but the LMS would only show that one brief login. Or I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes before an exam for hours without touching the platform at all. My best studying often happened completely disconnected from the internet.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd grab the PDFs and readings in one session, then work through them over several days without logging back in. It felt more efficient to have everything saved locally so I didn't need wifi or have to navigate the LMS every time. Once I had the materials, I didn't see the point in going back unless something new was posted.
Q12: Mid-semester was really rough for me. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started feeling really bored and disconnected from the material. There were weeks where just thinking about logging in made me feel overwhelmed, so I'd avoid it entirely. The CBT sessions I did helped me work through some of that, but engagement definitely dipped when my mental state was bad.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or had other things competing for my attention, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about assignments sometimes made me avoid them entirely rather than asking for help—my anxiety made reaching out feel impossible. Fatigue was a big one too; I'd open the platform with good intentions and just stare at it without actually doing anything productive.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd scroll through materials or let a video play in the background while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I was too anxious to actually absorb anything, so I was just going through the motions. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they'd show up as 'engagement' in the system.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even the important ones. My anxiety would build up around them until they felt insurmountable, and avoiding them felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I knew it was making things worse, but there's this weird thing where knowing something is important makes it scarier to start. The CBT helped me recognize this pattern, at least.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, which probably looked chaotic from the outside. Without a deadline, I'd keep telling myself I'd do it tomorrow. The pressure was stressful but also weirdly necessary for me to function.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback would give me a temporary boost and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when it was constructive, would sometimes send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid the course for a bit. I tried to use feedback to improve, but emotionally it was hard to separate my work from my self-worth.
Q18: The structure felt a bit overwhelming with so many online courses happening at once. Having ten courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it probably deserved. The way things were organized on the LMS didn't always make it clear what was most important, so I'd sometimes miss things or prioritize wrong. A clearer structure would have helped me manage my anxiety better.
Q19: The reading-heavy format actually worked well for my learning style, which was a plus. But the lack of clear prioritization made engagement harder—everything seemed equally important, which made everything feel equally overwhelming. More guidance on what to focus on first would have made it easier to start without feeling paralyzed by choices.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work, or someone who's confused and searching for answers without finding them. It might also be someone who's going through the motions to feel productive without actually being able to focus. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety in disguise.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I didn't log in much, but I was studying from downloaded materials and my own notes pretty consistently. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was low because my real learning happened offline. The system can't see the hours I spent with printed readings and handwritten notes.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do while walking around, the discussions with friends about course material. They definitely can't capture whether someone was mentally present or just going through the motions while anxious or bored.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when the material connects with you somehow and you want to know more. For me, real engagement is rare but powerful—it's when my anxiety quiets down enough that I can actually absorb and think critically about something.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop better coping strategies. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline studying or the internal struggle to even open the platform some days. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling bored and disconnected much of the time, I was still trying to climb my way through and actually learned things that mattered to me.
